blocks.text: But Judith said, “I cannot partake of them, or it will be an offense; but I will have enough with the things I brought with me.”
Holofernes said to her, “If your supply runs out, where can we get you more of the same? For none of your people are here with us.”
Judith replied, “As surely as you live, my lord, your servant will not use up the supplies I have with me before the Lord carries out by my hand what he has determined.”
Then the servants of Holofernes brought her into the tent, and she slept until midnight. Toward the morning watch she got up
and sent this message to Holofernes: “Let my lord now give orders to allow your servant to go out and pray.”
So Holofernes commanded his guards not to hinder her. She remained in the camp three days. She went out each night to the valley of Bethulia, and bathed at the spring in the camp.
After bathing, she prayed the Lord God of Israel to direct her way for the triumph of his people.
8 : Her daily is similar to rituals practiced at Qumran.
Then she returned purified and stayed in the tent until she ate her food toward evening.
12.1–9: Judith's daily schedule within Holofernes's camp. Judith imposes her schedule on Holofernes, which allows her to keep the dietary laws and pray.
On the fourth day Holofernes held a banquet for his personal attendants only, and did not invite any of his officers.
He said to Bagoas, the eunuch who had charge of his personal affairs, “Go and persuade the Hebrew woman who is in your care to join us and to eat and drink with us.
For it would be a disgrace if we let such a woman go without having intercourse with her. If we do not seduce her, she will laugh at us.”

So Bagoas left the presence of Holofernes, and approached her and said, “Let this pretty girl not hesitate to come to my lord to be honored in his presence, and to enjoy drinking wine with us, and to become today like one of the Assyrian women who serve in the palace of Nebuchadnezzar.”

11–13 : See 2.4n. Both Holofernes and Bagoas are putty in Judith's hands, an ironic contrast with the power they think they have. In Greek novels the beautiful heroine is often threatened with the prospect of being forced to marry a king or become a courtesan, but here Judith is fully in charge and manipulates the suggestion.
Judith replied, “Who am I to refuse my lord? Whatever pleases him I will do at once, and it will be a joy to me until the day of my death.”

So she proceeded to dress herself in all her woman's finery. Her maid went ahead and spread for her on the ground before Holofernes the lambskins she had received from Bagoas for her daily use in reclining.
15 : The lambskins may symbolically protect Judith from impurity; cf. Josephus, 3.270, 2.4.
Then Judith came in and lay down. Holofernes’ heart was ravished with her and his passion was aroused, for he had been waiting for an opportunity to seduce her from the day he first saw her.
So Holofernes said to her, “Have a drink and be merry with us!”
Judith said, “I will gladly drink, my lord, because today is the greatest day in my whole life.”
Then she took what her maid had prepared and ate and drank before him.
Holofernes was greatly pleased with her, and drank a great quantity of wine, much more than he had ever drunk in any one day since he was born.
8.1–16.25: Part II: Judith rescues her people.
12.10–20: Holofernes invites Judith to a banquet.

16–20 : Holofernes thinks that he will seduce Judith, but it is who seduces into drinking before. She seems as excited as Holofernes, but for an entirely different reason. Again, Judith speaks the truth that she intends to be misunderstood by Holofernes.
